question: What is the transformer architecture and how does it differ from RNNs?
idealAnswerCore: The transformer is an attention-based neural network architecture that processes sequences in parallel using self-attention, unlike RNNs which process tokens sequentially.
idealAnswerFraming: In interviews, emphasize that transformers solved the sequential bottleneck of RNNs — enabling massive parallelization during training and eliminating vanishing gradients over long sequences.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Transformers use self-attention to relate all tokens to each other simultaneously, giving O(n²) attention but O(1) path length between any two tokens
RNNs have O(n) path length between distant tokens, causing vanishing/exploding gradients and poor long-range dependency modeling
Transformers enable full parallelization during training; RNNs are inherently sequential
The encoder-decoder architecture (original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper) processes input and generates output; decoder-only models (GPT family) are most common for language generation
Positional encoding is required since transformers have no inherent notion of order
idealAnswerFollowups: How does multi-head attention extend single-head attention?
What are the trade-offs between encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder-decoder architectures?

question: Explain how self-attention works. What is Q, K, and V?
idealAnswerCore: Self-attention computes a weighted sum of values (V) where weights are determined by the compatibility (dot product) between queries (Q) and keys (K) from the same sequence.
idealAnswerFraming: Walk through the math: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ/√dₖ)V. Explain that Q/K/V are linear projections of the same input, allowing each token to attend to all others.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Q (query) represents what a token is looking for; K (key) represents what a token offers; V (value) is the information to aggregate
Dot product of Q and K measures compatibility; dividing by √dₖ prevents softmax saturation in high dimensions
Softmax converts scores to a probability distribution over positions
The output is a weighted sum of V vectors — each position gets a blended representation from all positions it attended to
In self-attention, Q, K, V all come from the same sequence; in cross-attention (encoder-decoder), Q comes from the decoder and K, V from the encoder
idealAnswerFollowups: Why do we scale by √dₖ?
What is multi-head attention and why is it useful?

question: What is multi-head attention and why does it help compared to single-head attention?
idealAnswerCore: Multi-head attention runs multiple attention computations in parallel with different learned projections, then concatenates the results — allowing the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces.
idealAnswerFraming: The key insight is that a single attention head collapses all information into one representation. Multiple heads let the model attend to syntactic relations, semantic similarity, and positional patterns simultaneously.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Each head projects Q, K, V into lower-dimensional subspaces (dₖ = d_model/h), so total compute stays the same as single-head
Different heads learn to attend to different aspects: one might capture syntax, another coreference, another semantic relatedness
Results from all heads are concatenated and projected back to d_model
In practice, heads specialize but this specialization is emergent, not explicitly trained
h=8 or h=16 are common choices; larger models use more heads
idealAnswerFollowups: How is multi-query attention (MQA) different from multi-head attention?
What is grouped-query attention (GQA) and why is it used in LLaMA?

question: What is the KV cache and how does it speed up inference?
idealAnswerCore: The KV cache stores the key and value tensors computed for previously processed tokens so they don't need to be recomputed during autoregressive generation.
idealAnswerFraming: During autoregressive generation, each new token needs attention over all previous tokens. Without caching, K and V for the entire context would be recomputed on every step — O(n²) total cost. KV cache reduces this to O(n) amortized.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: During the prefill phase, K and V for all input tokens are computed and cached
During the decode phase, only the new token's Q, K, V are computed; the new K, V are appended to the cache
Memory cost is 2 × num_layers × num_heads × head_dim × context_length × bytes_per_element
KV cache size scales with batch_size × context_length, becoming the primary memory bottleneck for long-context inference
Techniques like MQA and GQA reduce KV cache size by sharing K, V heads across query heads
idealAnswerFollowups: How does the KV cache affect the memory requirements for serving LLMs?
What is paged attention (vLLM) and how does it solve KV cache memory fragmentation?

question: Explain BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding) tokenization. What are its trade-offs?
idealAnswerCore: BPE is a subword tokenization algorithm that starts with individual characters and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs, creating a vocabulary of subword units that balances vocabulary size with coverage.
idealAnswerFraming: Tokenization is the foundation of how LLMs process text. BPE's main trade-off is between vocabulary size (affects embedding table memory and out-of-vocabulary handling) and tokenization efficiency (token count per character).
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Starts with a character-level vocabulary; iteratively merges most frequent adjacent token pairs until vocabulary size target is reached
Common words become single tokens; rare words are split into subword pieces; completely unknown words split to byte-level in some implementations
Trade-offs: larger vocab → fewer tokens per text (more efficient) but larger embedding table; smaller vocab → more robust to novel words but longer sequences
BPE can produce different tokenizations for the same content depending on training corpus — e.g., 'GPT-4' may not tokenize cleanly
Whitespace handling varies: GPT uses a leading-space marker (Ġ); SentencePiece uses a different convention affecting multi-language performance
idealAnswerFollowups: How does tokenization affect model performance on code vs. natural language?
What is the difference between BPE and WordPiece (used in BERT)?

question: What is quantization in the context of LLMs, and what are the main types?
idealAnswerCore: Quantization reduces the precision of model weights (and optionally activations) from float32/float16 to lower-bit representations like INT8 or INT4, reducing memory footprint and increasing inference speed with minimal quality loss.
idealAnswerFraming: For production LLM deployment, quantization is essential for cost efficiency. A 70B model in FP16 requires ~140GB VRAM; in INT4 it fits in ~35GB — enabling deployment on fewer, cheaper GPUs.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Post-training quantization (PTQ): quantize after training with no retraining; fast but can degrade quality for aggressive quantization
Quantization-aware training (QAT): simulates low-precision during training; higher quality but requires training compute
Weight-only quantization (GPTQ, AWQ, bitsandbytes): quantizes weights only, keeping activations in float16 — most common for LLM serving
INT8 typically has <1% quality degradation; INT4 can lose 2-5% depending on method and model size
GGUF format (llama.cpp) packages quantized models with metadata for efficient CPU/GPU inference
idealAnswerFollowups: What is the difference between GPTQ and AWQ quantization methods?
How does quantization interact with the KV cache?

question: How does speculative decoding work and what are the conditions for it to improve throughput?
idealAnswerCore: Speculative decoding uses a small 'draft' model to generate several candidate tokens in parallel, then a large 'target' model verifies them in a single forward pass — accepting correct tokens and rejecting at the first mismatch.
idealAnswerFraming: In interviews for inference optimization roles, explain that speculative decoding turns the single-token bottleneck into a token-batch problem, leveraging the fact that verification is much cheaper than generation when multiple tokens can be batched.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Draft model generates k tokens autoregressively; target model evaluates all k+1 positions in one forward pass (parallelizable)
Tokens are accepted if the target model agrees; at the first rejection, a corrected token is sampled from the target model
Speedup depends on acceptance rate α and draft model speed; effective speedup ≈ (1 - αᵏ⁺¹)/(1-α) tokens per target forward pass
Conditions for speedup: draft model must be much faster (smaller) than target; acceptance rate must be reasonably high (>0.7)
Works best when generating predictable text (code, structured data); less effective for highly creative generation
Medusa and similar architectures add parallel prediction heads to avoid a separate draft model
idealAnswerFollowups: How does speculative decoding change memory requirements?
What is the difference between speculative decoding and parallel decoding?

question: Explain Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture. What are its advantages and challenges?
idealAnswerCore: MoE replaces the dense FFN layer with multiple 'expert' FFN sub-networks and a learned router that selects a sparse subset (typically top-k=2) of experts per token — achieving larger model capacity with sublinear compute scaling.
idealAnswerFraming: MoE is the architecture behind GPT-4, Mixtral, and many frontier models. The key pitch is that you get the expressiveness of a 100B+ parameter model while only activating ~10-15B parameters per forward pass.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Each token is independently routed to k experts (typically 2 out of 8-64); only those experts are computed
Total parameters >> active parameters: Mixtral 8x7B has 47B parameters but uses ~13B per token
Router uses a learned linear projection + softmax to produce expert weights; tokens may be processed by different expert sets
Load balancing is critical: without auxiliary loss, routers collapse to few experts — auxiliary load-balancing loss encourages uniform expert utilization
Communication overhead: in distributed training/inference, tokens may need to be sent across GPUs to reach their experts (all-to-all communication)
Challenges: expert collapse, load imbalance, and increased memory vs. dense models of same active compute
idealAnswerFollowups: How do you handle load balancing in MoE during training?
What is the difference between token-choice and expert-choice routing?

question: What is the difference between encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder-decoder transformer architectures? When would you use each?
idealAnswerCore: Encoder-only models (BERT) produce rich bidirectional representations for classification/retrieval; decoder-only models (GPT) generate text autoregressively; encoder-decoder models (T5, BART) are suited for sequence-to-sequence tasks like translation and summarization.
idealAnswerFraming: In practice today, decoder-only models dominate generative AI. Explain the causal masking that makes decoder-only generation work, and when you'd still reach for an encoder-only model (embeddings, classification).
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Encoder-only: bidirectional attention — each token attends to all others; ideal for producing representations used in classification, NER, embeddings
Decoder-only: causal (left-to-right) masking — each token only attends to previous tokens; required for autoregressive text generation

Encoder-decoder: encoder produces contextualized representation; decoder generates output attending to encoder output via cross-attention; optimal for translation, summarization, code-completion with long context
Modern trend: instruction-tuned decoder-only models (GPT-4, Claude, Llama) handle most tasks that previously required encoder-decoder models
Encoder-only models still preferred for dense retrieval embeddings (lower latency, better for similarity search)
idealAnswerFollowups: Why don't we use encoder-only models for generation?
What is prefix LM and how does it combine encoder and decoder properties?

question: What is Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) and why has it become the dominant positional encoding approach?
idealAnswerCore: RoPE encodes position by rotating the Q and K vectors in the attention computation by an angle proportional to position, enabling relative position information to emerge naturally from the dot product — while generalizing to longer sequences than seen during training.
idealAnswerFraming: RoPE replaced learned absolute positional embeddings in most modern LLMs (Llama, Mistral, Falcon) because it enables length extrapolation and can be extended via techniques like YaRN and RoPE scaling.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Applies a rotation matrix to Q and K based on token position: Rₘq · Rₙk = R(m-n)(q·k) — the dot product depends only on relative position m-n
Unlike sinusoidal or learned absolute PE, RoPE naturally encodes relative positions in the attention score
Supports extrapolation beyond training context length with interpolation techniques (Position Interpolation, YaRN, LongRoPE)
Compatible with efficient attention implementations (Flash Attention) without modification
Widely adopted: LLaMA, Mistral, Falcon, Phi, Gemma all use RoPE
idealAnswerFollowups: How does RoPE compare to ALiBi for length extrapolation?
What is YaRN and how does it extend RoPE context length?

question: Explain Flash Attention. What problem does it solve and how?
idealAnswerCore: Flash Attention is an IO-aware exact attention algorithm that computes standard attention while minimizing HBM (GPU memory) reads/writes by using SRAM for tiled computation — achieving the same result as standard attention but with O(n) memory instead of O(n²).
idealAnswerFraming: Flash Attention is a practical engineering breakthrough: it makes long-context attention feasible without approximation. For serving infrastructure roles, understanding why memory bandwidth (not FLOPs) is the bottleneck is key.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Standard attention materializes the full n×n attention matrix in HBM — O(n²) memory and many slow HBM reads/writes
Flash Attention splits Q, K, V into tiles, computes attention locally in SRAM, and uses the online softmax trick to maintain numerical correctness without materializing the full matrix
Memory complexity reduced from O(n²) to O(n); wall-clock speedup from reduced HBM bandwidth (not reduced FLOPs — FLOPs are the same)
Flash Attention 2 further optimizes parallelism across sequence dimension and reduces non-matmul FLOPs
Flash Attention 3 adds support for hardware features in H100 (asynchronous execution, FP8)
Enables practical training/inference with context lengths of 100K+ tokens
idealAnswerFollowups: What is the online softmax trick used in Flash Attention?
How does Flash Attention interact with the KV cache?

question: What is temperature in LLM sampling and how does it affect outputs?
idealAnswerCore: Temperature scales the logits before the softmax in token sampling: lower temperature makes the distribution sharper (more deterministic); higher temperature makes it flatter (more diverse/random).
idealAnswerFraming: Temperature is the most common generation parameter you'll tune in practice. Explain that temperature=0 is greedy decoding, and that the right temperature depends on the task (0.2 for factual Q&A, 0.8-1.0 for creative generation).
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Temperature T divides logits before softmax: p(xᵢ) ∝ exp(logit_i / T)
T→0: argmax decoding (greedy); T=1: original model distribution; T>1: uniform distribution over vocabulary
Low temperature appropriate for factual, deterministic tasks; high temperature for brainstorming, creative writing
Top-p (nucleus) and top-k sampling complement temperature by limiting the candidate token pool
Repetition penalty can help at low temperatures where models get stuck in loops
idealAnswerFollowups: What is the difference between top-p and top-k sampling?
How does temperature interact with RLHF fine-tuning?

question: What are the compute and memory requirements for training a 7B parameter model from scratch?
idealAnswerCore: Training a 7B model from scratch on 1T tokens requires roughly 14 × 10¹⁸ FLOPs (Chinchilla scaling: ~6× params × tokens), approximately 14,000 A100-hours at ~0.5 efficiency, and requires ~30-50 GPU clusters with NVLink/InfiniBand interconnects.
idealAnswerFraming: This question tests whether you understand the economics and infrastructure reality of LLM training. Show you understand Chinchilla scaling, memory requirements per GPU, and the distributed training setup needed.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Memory for 7B model: ~14GB in FP16 (2 bytes/param); with optimizer states (Adam = 8 bytes/param) and gradients (~28GB total for parameters+gradients+optimizer) you need 70-90GB just for model state — distributed across GPUs
Chinchilla optimal tokens ≈ 20× parameters; so 7B model → 140B tokens optimal; modern models train on 1-2T tokens for stronger performance
FLOPs estimate: ~6 × N × D where N=params, D=tokens; 6 × 7×10⁹ × 10¹² ≈ 4×10²² FLOPs
A100 at ~300 TFLOPS effective throughput (MFU ~40%): ~130M GPU-hours — wait, 4×10²² / (0.3×10¹⁵ × 3600 × 0.4) ≈ 10⁶ GPU-seconds ≈ a few hundred A100-days
Data parallelism, tensor parallelism, and pipeline parallelism all required at 7B+ scale
idealAnswerFollowups: What is MFU (Model Flops Utilization) and why is achieving >40% difficult?
How does ZeRO optimizer reduce memory requirements?

question: What is RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) and why is it used instead of just relying on the LLM's parametric knowledge?
idealAnswerCore: RAG augments LLM generation with information retrieved from an external knowledge base at inference time — grounding responses in specific, up-to-date documents rather than relying solely on knowledge baked into model weights during training.
idealAnswerFraming: RAG is the dominant architecture for enterprise AI applications. Lead with the core limitations it solves: knowledge cutoff dates, hallucination on specific facts, and the cost/risk of retraining for new information.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: LLMs hallucinate on specific facts, proprietary data, and post-training information; RAG grounds answers in retrieved evidence
RAG is dramatically cheaper than retraining or fine-tuning for adding new knowledge
Retrieved documents are included in the context window, making the model's reasoning auditable and attributable to sources
Trade-offs: RAG adds retrieval latency and depends on retrieval quality; if retrieval fails, generation quality degrades
Common alternatives: fine-tuning (expensive, doesn't solve hallucination), long-context models (expensive, doesn't always help with retrieval)
idealAnswerFollowups: When would you choose fine-tuning over RAG?
How do you evaluate whether your RAG system is actually using the retrieved context?

question: What is a vector database? How does it differ from a traditional relational database?
idealAnswerCore: A vector database stores high-dimensional embedding vectors and supports efficient approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search, enabling semantic similarity queries that relational databases (optimized for exact match and range queries) cannot perform efficiently.
idealAnswerFraming: For a production RAG system, the vector DB is the retrieval backbone. Explain that relational DBs use B-tree indexes optimized for equality/range queries; vector DBs use HNSW or IVF indexes optimized for cosine/dot-product similarity in 768-4096 dimensional spaces.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Traditional DB: exact match, range queries via B-tree/hash indexes; doesn't scale to high-dimensional similarity search
Vector DB: ANN search using HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs) or IVF (Inverted File Index) — trades small recall loss for massive speed gains
Common vector DBs: Pinecone (managed), Weaviate (open-source), Qdrant (open-source, Rust), Chroma (lightweight), pgvector (Postgres extension for prototyping)
Vector DBs also support metadata filtering (pre-filter or post-filter) to combine semantic search with structured constraints
At small scale (<100K vectors), simple numpy similarity search is often faster and simpler than a dedicated vector DB
idealAnswerFollowups: What is HNSW and what are its trade-offs vs. flat search?
How would you choose between Pinecone, Qdrant, and pgvector for a production system?

question: Explain different chunking strategies for RAG. How do you choose chunk size?
idealAnswerCore: Chunking splits documents into segments for embedding and indexing; the optimal strategy depends on document structure, query type, and the embedding model's context window — balancing semantic coherence against retrieval granularity.
idealAnswerFraming: Chunking is often the biggest lever for RAG quality improvement that practitioners underestimate. Show you understand the precision-recall tradeoff: small chunks → higher precision but may miss context; large chunks → more context but diluted embeddings.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Fixed-size chunking: simple, consistent; ignores semantic structure; chunk_size 256-512 tokens is a common starting point
Sentence/paragraph splitting: respects natural semantic boundaries; better coherence but variable size
Recursive character text splitting: splits on paragraph → sentence → word boundaries until within size limit (LangChain default approach)
Semantic chunking: clusters sentences with similar embeddings into chunks; highest quality but adds compute
Chunk overlap (10-20%): prevents information loss at boundaries; increases index size
Parent-child chunking: embed small child chunks for precision, retrieve parent chunk for full context; good balance
idealAnswerFollowups: What is the 'lost in the middle' problem and how does chunk size affect it?
How do you handle chunking for structured documents like PDFs with tables?

question: What is hybrid search in RAG? When should you use it over pure dense retrieval?
idealAnswerCore: Hybrid search combines dense vector similarity search (semantic) with sparse keyword search (BM25/TF-IDF), typically via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) — capturing both semantic meaning and exact lexical matches.
idealAnswerFraming: Pure dense retrieval misses exact keyword matches that users expect to work (product names, codes, jargon). Hybrid search is the production default for most enterprise RAG systems because it handles both cases gracefully.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Dense retrieval: captures semantic similarity; fails on exact rare terms (SKU numbers, version strings, technical acronyms not in training data)
BM25 sparse retrieval: exact keyword match; captures technical terms, proper nouns; misses paraphrases and semantic variants
Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF): score = Σ 1/(k+rank_i) per system; simple, parameter-free, robust to score scale differences
Alternative fusion: weighted linear combination of normalized scores; requires tuning and scale normalization
Use cases for pure dense: general Q&A over well-written prose; use hybrid when: queries contain specific codes, names, technical terms; users complain about exact matches not appearing
idealAnswerFollowups: How do you tune the weights between dense and sparse in hybrid search?
What is ColBERT and how does it differ from bi-encoder approaches?

question: What is reranking in RAG and when should you use a cross-encoder vs. a bi-encoder?
idealAnswerCore: Reranking is a two-stage retrieval pattern where a fast first-stage retriever (bi-encoder) fetches top-k candidates, then a slower but more accurate reranker (cross-encoder) scores query-document pairs jointly to improve the final ranking.
idealAnswerFraming: This is a classic efficiency-accuracy tradeoff question. Bi-encoders precompute document embeddings; cross-encoders see query and document together. Explain why you'd never use a cross-encoder for first-stage retrieval at scale.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Bi-encoder: encodes query and document independently; enables precomputed document embeddings; fast ANN retrieval; lower accuracy because it can't model query-document interaction
Cross-encoder: concatenates query+document, applies full attention across both; high accuracy because it models interaction; O(n) cost per query — cannot precompute
Typical pipeline: bi-encoder retrieves top 100 → cross-encoder reranks → top 5-10 passed to LLM
Common cross-encoders: Cohere Rerank API, BGE-Reranker, MS-MARCO trained cross-encoders
Reranking adds 50-200ms latency but can improve RAG answer quality significantly for complex queries
idealAnswerFollowups: How would you evaluate whether your reranker is actually helping?
What is ColBERT and how does late interaction compare to full cross-encoder attention?

question: How do you evaluate a RAG system? What metrics and frameworks do you use?
idealAnswerCore: RAG evaluation must assess both retrieval quality (are the right documents retrieved?) and generation quality (does the answer faithfully use the retrieved context?) — typically using frameworks like RAGAS or TruLens that automate LLM-as-judge evaluations.
idealAnswerFraming: Evaluation is the most critical and underinvested part of RAG development. Show you understand you need a golden dataset and metrics for both retrieval and generation, and that you can't evaluate just one without the other.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Retrieval metrics: Recall@k (did the right docs make it into top-k?), MRR (mean reciprocal rank), NDCG
Generation metrics: Faithfulness (is the answer grounded in retrieved docs?), Answer Relevance (does it address the question?), Context Precision/Recall (RAGAS)
RAGAS: automated evaluation framework using LLM-as-judge for faithfulness, answer relevance, context precision/recall without human labels
TruLens: evaluation and tracing framework; records the full RAG pipeline and scores each component
Human eval: ultimately required for production sign-off; costly but necessary to validate automated metrics
Build a golden test set: 50-200 question-answer-source_document triples; evaluate retrieval and generation separately
idealAnswerFollowups: How do you detect and measure hallucination in RAG outputs?
What is the difference between context precision and context recall in RAGAS?

question: Design a RAG system for a legal firm with 10M documents. Walk through your architecture.
idealAnswerCore: A production-scale legal RAG system requires a multi-stage architecture: offline document processing pipeline, scalable vector index, hybrid retrieval, and a compliance-aware generation layer with source attribution.
idealAnswerFraming: This is a system design question. Structure your answer: data ingestion → indexing → retrieval → generation → serving. Call out scale-specific challenges: index size, update latency, access control, and latency requirements.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Ingestion: document parsing (PDF → text via OCR for scanned docs), chunking (legal docs need section-aware chunking to preserve case citations), metadata extraction (date, court, jurisdiction, case number)
Indexing: 10M documents × ~5 chunks/doc = 50M vectors; sharded vector index across multiple nodes; consider Qdrant or Weaviate distributed mode; Elasticsearch + vector plugin for hybrid
Retrieval: mandatory hybrid (BM25 for case citations, statute numbers + dense for semantic); mandatory reranking; access control at retrieval layer (row-level security by user permissions)
Generation: GPT-4/Claude with strict citation requirements; every claim must cite retrieved source with page/section; hallucination guardrails
Serving: <2s P95 latency SLA; pre-warm popular query paths; streaming generation for user responsiveness
Cost at scale: 50M embeddings × 1536 dims × 4 bytes ≈ 300GB index memory; requires dedicated GPU/memory-optimized nodes
idealAnswerFollowups: How would you handle document updates when new cases are filed?
How do you handle access control to ensure users only retrieve documents they're permitted to see?

question: What is HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) and when would you use it?
idealAnswerCore: HyDE generates a hypothetical answer to the query using an LLM, then embeds that hypothetical answer (rather than the original query) as the retrieval vector — bridging the vocabulary and style gap between short queries and long document passages.
idealAnswerFraming: HyDE addresses the query-document mismatch problem: a user query like 'why does attention scale poorly?' is stylistically very different from a paper passage explaining O(n²) complexity. The hypothetical document is written in 'document style' and retrieves more relevant passages.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Standard dense retrieval embeds the query and finds similar document chunks; queries are often sparse and short, documents are dense and long — style mismatch hurts recall
HyDE generates a hypothetical passage that 'would answer the query' then retrieves on that passage's embedding
Works best when: queries are short/ambiguous; documents are technical/long; the LLM has relevant domain knowledge to generate a plausible hypothesis
Risks: if the LLM's hypothetical document is factually wrong or off-topic, retrieval will fail; adds one LLM call to latency
When to use: low recall on complex technical queries; not worth it when queries are already verbose and specific
idealAnswerFollowups: How does HyDE compare to query expansion?
When would you combine HyDE with reranking?

question: What is an embedding model and how is it different from the LLM used for generation in RAG?
idealAnswerCore: An embedding model is an encoder neural network that maps text to a fixed-dimensional dense vector representing its semantic meaning; it's separate from the generation LLM and optimized for similarity comparison rather than text generation.
idealAnswerFraming: Many engineers confuse the embedding model with the generation model. Make clear that they serve different purposes: the embedding model populates and queries the vector index; the generation LLM synthesizes the final answer.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Embedding models (e.g., text-embedding-3-large, BGE-large, E5-large) are typically smaller, encoder-only transformers optimized via contrastive learning (SimCSE, E5 training) to cluster similar texts nearby in embedding space
Output is a fixed-size vector (768, 1024, or 1536 dims typically) regardless of input length
The generation LLM (GPT-4, Claude, etc.) takes query + retrieved chunks as context and generates a natural language answer
They can come from different providers: you might use Cohere embeddings with Claude for generation
Key metric for embedding models: recall on MTEB (Massive Text Embedding Benchmark) for your use case
idealAnswerFollowups: How do you choose an embedding model for a domain-specific RAG system?
What is the MTEB benchmark and which dimensions of it matter for RAG?

question: What is fine-tuning and when should you use it vs. RAG vs. prompting?
idealAnswerCore: Fine-tuning adapts a pre-trained model to a specific task or style by continuing training on a targeted dataset, updating model weights — appropriate when you need consistent behavior, style, or knowledge that can't be achieved through prompting or retrieval alone.
idealAnswerFraming: The key decision framework in interviews: use prompting first (cheapest), then RAG for knowledge, then fine-tuning for behavior/style. Fine-tuning is most justified when you need consistent output format, domain style, or to compress many few-shot examples into weights.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Fine-tuning for behavior/style: teaching a model to always respond in JSON, follow a specific persona, use domain-specific terminology
Fine-tuning for task: classification, NER, code generation in a niche language — where in-context examples don't generalize well
RAG is better for knowledge (proprietary docs, real-time data); fine-tuning doesn't prevent hallucination and is expensive to update
Prompting first: if 5-10 well-crafted examples in the prompt achieve the desired behavior, avoid fine-tuning
Cost hierarchy: prompting (free) → RAG (vector DB cost) → fine-tuning (training compute + hosting specialized model)
idealAnswerFollowups: How does instruction fine-tuning differ from domain fine-tuning?
What is catastrophic forgetting and how do you mitigate it during fine-tuning?

question: What is LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) and how does it make fine-tuning more efficient?
idealAnswerCore: LoRA adds small trainable low-rank decomposition matrices (A and B) to frozen pre-trained weight matrices, training only these adapters instead of all model weights — reducing trainable parameters by 10-10,000× while achieving comparable performance to full fine-tuning.
idealAnswerFraming: LoRA is the dominant fine-tuning technique in practice. For a 7B model, full fine-tuning requires 70GB+ VRAM; LoRA can fine-tune on a single 24GB GPU. Show you understand the math and the hyperparameter choices.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: For a weight matrix W ∈ ℝᵐˣⁿ, LoRA adds ΔW = BA where B ∈ ℝᵐˣʳ, A ∈ ℝʳˣⁿ and rank r << min(m,n)
Typically applied to Q, K, V, and output projection matrices in attention; r=8-64 is common
Trainable parameters: r×(m+n) instead of m×n; for a 4096×4096 matrix with r=8: 65K vs 16M parameters
At inference, ΔW can be merged into W (W' = W + αBA/r) for zero additional latency
α (scaling factor) and r (rank) are key hyperparameters; α/r ratio affects strength of adaptation
QLoRA combines LoRA with 4-bit quantized base model, enabling 7B+ fine-tuning on 16GB GPU
idealAnswerFollowups: What is the difference between LoRA and full fine-tuning in terms of final model quality?
How do you choose the rank r in LoRA?

question: What is QLoRA and how does it reduce memory requirements compared to LoRA?
idealAnswerCore: QLoRA fine-tunes a base model that is quantized to 4-bit (NF4) precision, with LoRA adapters trained in BF16 — dramatically reducing base model memory while maintaining fine-tuning quality through double quantization and paged optimizers.
idealAnswerFraming: QLoRA is the reason consumer GPUs can fine-tune 7B-13B models. The key innovation is treating the 4-bit quantized model as a frozen base and learning adapters at full precision — decoupling the base model precision from the adapter training precision.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: NF4 (4-bit NormalFloat): a new data type optimized for normally-distributed weights (as LLM weights tend to be); better than INT4 for weight quantization
Double quantization: quantizes the quantization constants themselves, saving ~0.37 bits/parameter additionally
Paged optimizers: uses NVIDIA unified memory to offload optimizer states to CPU RAM during memory spikes, preventing OOM errors
Full fine-tuning 7B model: ~112GB VRAM (model + optimizer + gradients); QLoRA 7B: ~10-12GB VRAM on consumer GPU
Accuracy: QLoRA achieves within 1% of full fine-tuning on most benchmarks per original paper
idealAnswerFollowups: What are the compute trade-offs of QLoRA vs. standard LoRA?
How do you convert a QLoRA-trained model back to a full-precision model for merging?

question: What is RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)? Walk through the standard three-stage pipeline.
idealAnswerCore: RLHF is a training paradigm that uses human preference data to train a reward model, then uses that reward model as a signal to fine-tune a language model via reinforcement learning — aligning the model with human values and preferences.
idealAnswerFraming: RLHF is how ChatGPT and Claude were trained to be helpful and harmless. The key insight is that humans can reliably rank outputs without being able to specify the reward function explicitly. Show you understand all three stages and their failure modes.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Stage 1 — Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT): fine-tune base model on high-quality demonstration data to get a well-behaved starting point
Stage 2 — Reward Model Training: collect human preferences (comparisons between two outputs); train a separate model to predict human-preferred output
Stage 3 — RL Fine-Tuning: optimize the SFT model using the reward model with PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization); add KL divergence penalty to prevent reward hacking/model collapse
Challenges: reward hacking (model learns to exploit the reward model), mode collapse, computational cost of PPO, instability
Human preference collection is expensive: Anthropic's Constitutional AI and OpenAI's InstructGPT required thousands of human hours
idealAnswerFollowups: What is reward hacking and how does the KL penalty prevent it?
How does DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) simplify the RLHF pipeline?

question: How does DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) differ from RLHF? What are its advantages and limitations?
idealAnswerCore: DPO reformulates RLHF as a supervised classification problem over preference pairs, implicitly optimizing the reward model without separate RL training — using a closed-form optimal policy to directly fine-tune the model on preference data.
idealAnswerFraming: DPO has largely replaced PPO-based RLHF in practice because it's simpler, more stable, and requires no separate reward model. However, it has failure modes that make it less suitable for complex alignment tasks.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: DPO derives that the optimal policy under the RLHF objective has a closed form: policy ∝ reference_policy × exp(r/β); this allows reformulating reward learning as a classification loss on preference pairs
Loss: -log σ(β log(π(yw|x)/πref(yw|x)) - β log(π(yl|x)/πref(yl|x))) — maximizes probability of preferred over rejected
No separate reward model needed; no PPO stability issues; simpler to implement and tune
Requires a reference model (frozen base SFT model) during training; 2× memory vs. standard fine-tuning

Limitations: DPO can overfit to preferred responses and degrade on out-of-distribution inputs; doesn't handle multi-step or verifiable rewards well; RLHF with PPO remains better for complex reasoning tasks
ORPO (Odds Ratio Preference Optimization): eliminates the reference model requirement by using the current model's own probabilities
idealAnswerFollowups: When would you still choose PPO over DPO?
What is SimPO and how does it address DPO's reference model dependency?

question: What is catastrophic forgetting and how do you mitigate it during fine-tuning?
idealAnswerCore: Catastrophic forgetting occurs when fine-tuning on a new task causes a model to lose performance on previously learned capabilities — because gradient updates for the new task overwrite weights encoding prior knowledge.
idealAnswerFraming: Catastrophic forgetting is a practical blocker for domain-specific fine-tuning. Show you know the mitigation techniques and the trade-offs: LoRA prevents most forgetting; EWC adds complexity; data mixing is the pragmatic solution.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Root cause: SGD gradient updates for new data are globally applied to all weights, disrupting representations encoded during pretraining
PEFT methods (LoRA): adapters are additive; base model weights are frozen; no catastrophic forgetting by construction — most practical solution
Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC): adds regularization penalizing changes to weights important for prior tasks (estimated via Fisher information matrix); computationally expensive
Data mixing: include a fraction (~5-10%) of pretraining/general-purpose data in the fine-tuning dataset; simple and effective for maintaining general capabilities
Continual learning benchmarks: measure performance on both old tasks and new tasks after fine-tuning
Symptoms: after fine-tuning on code, model struggles with general instruction following; after fine-tuning on customer support, model loses world knowledge
idealAnswerFollowups: How do you measure catastrophic forgetting in a fine-tuned model?
When would you use full fine-tuning vs. PEFT to minimize forgetting?

question: What is supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and what data format does it typically use?
idealAnswerCore: SFT is fine-tuning a pretrained base model on high-quality (instruction, response) pairs to teach it to follow instructions, answer questions, and maintain a conversational format — the first stage of creating a helpful assistant from a base model.
idealAnswerFraming: SFT is the entry point to making a base model useful. Interviewers want to know you understand the data format, masking strategy, and the quality-vs-quantity tradeoff in SFT data.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Data format: instruction-response pairs, often in a chat template (e.g., ChatML: <|im_start|>user\n...); the model is trained to predict response tokens given instruction tokens
Loss masking: typically compute cross-entropy loss only on response tokens (not instruction tokens); prevents the model from learning to 'generate instructions'
Data quality >> data quantity: LIMA paper showed 1000 carefully curated examples outperformed 50K noisier examples for instruction following
Common data sources: OpenAssistant, Alpaca, ShareGPT, FLAN; commercial-grade models use human-written instruction-response pairs
Chat template matters: Llama3 uses a specific template with <|begin_of_text|> tags; training and inference templates must match or outputs degrade
idealAnswerFollowups: How do you curate SFT data for quality?
What is the impact of the chat template on fine-tuned model performance?
